svg.text: SWAHILI
HISTORY & EAST AFRICAN COAST
SWAHILI ORIGINS
Bantu speakers reach East African coast ~100-300 CE
Arab traders: contacts from ~700 CE; Arabic loanwords pour in
City-states: Mombasa, Malindi, Kilwa, Zanzibar, Sofala (900-1500 CE)
Swahili culture: uniquely African-Arab-Indian Ocean fusion
16th c.: Portuguese disrupt Indian Ocean trade networks
SWAHILI AS LINGUA FRANCA
19th c.: Arab-Swahili traders spread Swahili into interior
Slave and ivory trade routes: Swahili follows caravan paths
German East Africa, British East Africa: colonizers adopt Swahili
Today: 200M+ speakers across 14 countries (L1 + L2)
UN African Union, East African Community: Swahili is official
KIKUYU HISTORY · SECONDARY LANGUAGE
GIKUYU ORIGIN TRADITION
Gikuyu tradition: Ngai (God) gave first man and woman Mount Kenya
Kikuyu heartland: Central Kenya highlands around Mount Kenya
Settled agriculturalists: fig tree (mugomo) central to culture
Nine clans (mbari): traced to nine daughters of founder couple
COLONIAL PERIOD & MAU MAU
British colonists seized Kikuyu highlands (White Highlands)
1952-1960: Mau Mau uprising — Kikuyu-led anti-colonial revolt
Jomo Kenyatta: Kikuyu; first president of Kenya (1964-1978)
Ngugi wa Thiongo: world-renowned Kikuyu novelist and playwright
KIKUYU TODAY
~8M speakers; Kenya's largest ethnic group (~17% of population)
Nairobi surrounds traditional Kikuyu territory
Kikuyu economic dominance: nicknamed "the Jews of Africa" by some
SWAHILI-KIKUYU KENYA
Kenya: Swahili (national) + English (official) + Kikuyu (largest L1)
Nairobi: Swahili as urban lingua franca among all Kikuyu
Kikuyu learning Swahili: both within 300mi, both Bantu
Shared Bantu grammar gives Kikuyu speakers advantage with Swahili
Ngugi wa Thiongo: writes in Kikuyu as political act of decolonization
PLATE 20d ALT · SWAHILI / KIKUYU · HISTORY
